items.title: Always end with a structured debrief
items.avoid: 'Thanks, that was great!' and signing off.
items.prefer: 10-minute debrief: top strength, top weakness, one thing to drill.
items.why: Without the debrief, the diagnostic value of the mock is mostly wasted.
items.example:
```
Debrief prompts:
- 'Where did I lose you?'
- 'What would the strongest version of my approach look like?'
- 'If you were hiring me, would you?'
```

items.title: Budget each phase visibly
items.avoid: Letting exploration eat the coding budget.
items.prefer: Speak the budget aloud: 'I'll spend 5 minutes clarifying, then 5 outlining, then start coding'.
items.why: Verbal budgets keep you honest and signal mature time-management to the interviewer.
items.example: 'I've used 8 of my 10 outlining minutes — let me start coding the hash-map approach now.'

items.title: Rotate problem domains and partners
items.avoid: Drilling only the patterns you're comfortable with against only one partner.
items.prefer: Mix arrays, strings, graphs, DP, design; mix peer / senior / AI partners.
items.why: Comfort zones produce false confidence; rotation surfaces real weak spots.
items.example: Week plan: Mon arrays peer mock, Wed graph senior mock, Fri system-design self mock.

items.title: Record yourself periodically
items.avoid: Trusting your self-assessment after the fact.
items.prefer: Record audio/video every 3rd or 4th mock; watch at 1.5×.
items.why: Filler words, pauses, hand-waving and posture are obvious on tape and invisible in the moment.
items.example: Record into Loom / OBS; watch your own clarification phase first — that's usually the most revealing.

items.title: Drill the failure mode, not the solved problem
items.prefer: Spend the day after a mock drilling whatever broke (e.g. DP recurrences, tree traversal, graph BFS).
items.why: Repetition of strengths plateaus; targeted weakness drilling moves your floor.
items.example: Couldn't compose the DP recurrence cleanly → 1 fresh DP problem/day for a week, narrated aloud.
